title: Context-indexed dynamic feedback candidate
notes: Fourth-round candidate generated after critique of the dynamic stratified feedback candidate. It tests whether time-lagged feedback can remain explicit while conditioning community licensing, ideology, production, and judgment by community, norm centre, genre, medium, task frame, and speaker cue. It is not scored pending critique.

conditioning_axes.community: Required. Interpret community_licensing, production_probability, usage_frequency, and reported_acceptability relative to a specified speech community or comparison set.
conditioning_axes.norm_centre: Required when standard_language_ideology, metalinguistic_condemnation, or editorial_correction_probability are used. Do not treat the standard norm centre as the whole community.
conditioning_axes.genre: Required for register-sensitive cases. Specify the activity type before interpreting register_genre_appropriateness or production_probability.
conditioning_axes.medium: Required when speech, edited prose, messaging, classroom response, or test response may diverge.
conditioning_axes.task_framing: Required for reported_acceptability and grammaticality_attribution. Specify whether the task cues correctness, naturalness, register fit, or community membership.
conditioning_axes.speaker_identity: Required for socially indexed or stigmatized forms. Specify the speaker cue or matched-guise condition before interpreting reported_acceptability.

edges.rationale: Context-conditioned production at one slice contributes to observed usage in the matching context at the next slice.
Observed usage at t1 depends on the context-conditioned number of opportunities for the form.
Opportunity mass calibrates how strongly attestation or absence bears on licensing in the specified community and context.
Usage at t1 is evidence for licensing only inside the specified opportunity and community frame.
Acceptability at t1 can be a noisy measurement of licensing once context, ideology, and task frame are specified.
The specified norm centre can generate explicit condemnation at t1.
Condemnation at t1 predicts correction in the specified institutional or editorial context.
Correctness ideology can depress reported acceptability when the task or norm centre activates it.
Speaker cues shape the social-indexical value assigned to a form in the judgment context.
Indexical associations can shift reported acceptability without changing community licensing.
Genre determines which forms are appropriate for the activity type at t1.
Medium conditions whether a form is ordinary, marked, or correction-prone.
Register or genre mismatch can lower reported acceptability without changing licensing.
Task framing can cue correctness, naturalness, register fit, or community membership criteria.
Task framing can change explicit grammaticality attribution by cueing what kind of status is being requested.
Context-conditioned licensing is evidence for attribution, but not identical to attribution.
Acceptability reports provide noisy evidence for attribution in the specified task frame.
Correction can influence attribution while remaining separable from licensing and norm-centre ideology.
Licensing at t1 can increase later production in the same specified community and context.
Correction at t1 can suppress, redirect, or reshape later production in monitored contexts.
Acceptance or rejection at t1 can affect later production choices in comparable contexts.
Sustained context-conditioned usage at t1 contributes to later stabilization.
Community licensing at t1 contributes to later stabilization in that community.
Correction pressure at t1 can shape the route, spread, or delay of later stabilization.
Stabilization by t2 is evidence about licensing at t2 in the specified community.
Stabilization can shift later standard-language ideology in the specified norm centre.
Licensing at t2 affects production availability in the specified community and context.
Standard-language ideology at t2 can shape production choices in monitored contexts.

score_status.scope: Diachronic and context-conditioned licensing, ideology, correction, production, acceptability, and attribution module; not a general account.
